section.title: Unless the Lord Builds the House
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents of Solomon, eighth in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Solomon, the builder of the temple, confesses that every house, city, and family depends entirely on the LORD, not on anxious human effort.
section.thesis: All human labor, whether building a house, guarding a city, or raising children, is empty striving unless the LORD is its builder and giver, so His people are called to trust Him rather than toil anxiously, a truth ultimately fulfilled in Christ, who builds His church on a foundation no labor of man could lay.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the futility of anxious labor without the LORD, verses 1 to 2, and the blessing of children as a heritage from the LORD, verses 3 to 5.

units.label: Unless the LORD builds the house

verses.text: Unless the LORD builds the house, its builders labor in vain; unless the LORD protects the city, its watchmen stand guard in vain.
In vain you rise early and stay up late, toiling for bread to eat, for He gives sleep to His beloved.

units.label: Children are a heritage from the LORD

verses.text: Children are indeed a {{nachalah|heritage}} from the LORD, and the fruit of the womb is His reward.
Like arrows in the hand of a warrior, so are children born in one's youth.
Blessed is the man whose quiver is full of them. He will not be put to shame when he confronts the enemies at the gate.

nachalah.gloss: An inheritance or possession assigned as a lasting portion; children are called this, a heritage from the LORD given as a gift and reward, not a burden earned by effort.

basic.context: Attributed to Solomon, the builder of Israel's temple and fortifications, this Song of Ascents confesses that every human labor, house building, city guarding, and anxious toil, is empty unless the LORD is behind it, then turns to celebrate children as His good gift.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes the futility of self-reliant labor and the blessing of a full household, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern working diligently while trusting the LORD for the outcome, resting from anxious toil, and receiving children as His gift rather than a mere accomplishment.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm's key term is shav, vain or empty, repeated three times in verses 1 to 2 to hammer home the futility of labor without the LORD, before turning to nachalah, heritage, the gift-language used for children in verse 3.
basic.typology: The LORD as the true builder of the house anticipates Christ, who alone builds His church on an unshakeable foundation, and the children who defend their father at the gate anticipate believers who stand for Christ, the greater builder, against every opposing power.
basic.christ: Jesus declares, 'I will build My church, and the gates of Hades will not overpower it,' Matthew 16:18, directly echoing this psalm's confession that only the LORD's building endures, and He is Himself the wisdom by which every truly lasting house, family, and city is established, 1 Corinthians 3:11.
basic.god: The LORD alone makes any labor fruitful, whether building, guarding, or working for daily bread, and He is the one who gives children as a heritage and reward, gifts that bring security and honor to those who receive them from His hand rather than seize them by anxious striving.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 16:18 shows Christ as the builder whose church the gates of Hades cannot overcome. Hebrews 3:3-4 honors Christ above every human builder, since the builder of a house has more honor than the house itself, and God is the builder of all things. Ephesians 2:19-22 pictures believers built together into a dwelling place for God.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's confession that only the LORD's building endures is fulfilled in Christ, who builds His church on a foundation no human effort could lay, and gives His people, like children in a household, both security and purpose in Him.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents attributed to Solomon, built around the emphatic threefold repetition of 'in vain' in verses 1 to 2, before pivoting to the positive, celebratory tone of verses 3 to 5 on children as a heritage.
context.historical: Fittingly attributed to Solomon, who built the temple and fortified Jerusalem, yet was later warned that obedience, not architecture, secures a dynasty, 1 Kings 9, making this psalm read like his own distilled wisdom on where true security lies.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 126's promise that tearful sowing yields a joyful harvest with a companion truth, that even successful building and labor are empty unless the LORD is in them, and anticipates the New Testament's picture of Christ as the true and lasting builder.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach that human labor in building, guarding, and providing is futile apart from the LORD's blessing, freeing His people from anxious toil, and to celebrate children as a heritage and reward given by the LORD rather than achieved by human effort alone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes ancient Israelite building, city-watching, and family life. It prescribes as an abiding pattern working diligently while resting from anxious striving, trusting the LORD for the outcome of labor, and receiving children as a gift from His hand.

debates.question: Does verse 2's promise that the LORD 'gives sleep to His beloved' mean God grants restful sleep, or that God accomplishes good for His beloved while they sleep?

views.label: God grants restful sleep itself
views.case: The most natural reading is that the LORD gives sleep as a gift, contrasted with anxious toil that steals rest from those who do not trust Him.

views.label: God works on behalf of His beloved during their sleep
views.case: The Hebrew can be read as 'He gives to His beloved in sleep,' meaning God accomplishes good for His people even while they rest, without their frantic effort.

debates.bearing: Either reading teaches the same trust, that the LORD's blessing, not anxious wakefulness, secures what His people truly need.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm hammers home shav, vain or empty, three times in verses 1 to 2, before turning to nachalah, heritage, the language of a lasting, gift-given inheritance used for children in verse 3, framing family as grace received rather than achievement earned.

typology.type: The LORD as the true builder of the house, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who builds His church so that the gates of Hades cannot overpower it, Matthew 16:18.

typology.type: Children as arrows defending their father at the gate, verses 4 to 5
typology.fulfillment: Believers built up and equipped by Christ to stand firm for Him against every opposing power, Ephesians 6:10-13.

study.christ: Jesus takes up this psalm's very confession when He declares, 'I will build My church, and the gates of Hades will not overpower it,' Matthew 16:18, the true and lasting fulfillment of the truth that no house stands unless the LORD builds it. Hebrews 3:3-4 honors Christ above every human builder, for the builder of a house has more honor than the house itself, and it is God who built all things. In Him, believers become living stones built together into a dwelling place for God, Ephesians 2:19-22.
study.god: The LORD alone makes any labor fruitful, whether building a house, guarding a city, or laboring for daily bread, and it is He who gives children as a heritage and a reward, gifts of security and honor bestowed on those who receive them from His hand rather than seize them through anxious striving.

crossReferences.note: Christ declares He will build His church so that the gates of Hades cannot overpower it, the fulfillment of this psalm's confession.
Honors Christ above every human builder, since the builder of a house has more honor than the house, and God built all things.
Believers are built together into a dwelling place for God, the lasting house only the LORD, in Christ, can build.

study.oneStory: This psalm's confession that only the LORD's building endures is fulfilled in Christ, who builds His church on a foundation no human effort could ever lay, and who gives His people, like children within a household, both security and purpose found entirely in Him.

sermon.title: Don't Eat the Bread of Anxious Toil
sermon.why: Piper expounds Psalm 127:1-2 directly, showing how trusting that the LORD builds the house frees believers from the anxious toil this psalm warns against.

quiz.q: What does verse 1 say happens to builders and watchmen if the LORD is not in their labor?

options.text: Their building and guarding are done in vain.
options.note: This is the psalm's exact double claim, that labor without the LORD is empty.

options.text: They succeed even more quickly than expected.
options.note: The psalm states the opposite, that such labor is futile, not accelerated success.

options.text: They receive a smaller reward but still succeed.
options.note: The text says the labor is in vain, not merely reduced in reward.

options.text: They are guaranteed eventual success after delay.
options.note: The psalm gives no such guarantee apart from the LORD's involvement; it declares the labor vain.

quiz.q: According to verse 2, what does the LORD give to His beloved?

options.text: Sleep, in contrast to anxious, exhausting toil.
options.note: This is the specific gift named in verse 2, contrasted with rising early and staying up late in anxious labor.

options.text: Unlimited wealth regardless of effort.
options.note: The verse speaks of sleep and rest, not a promise of unlimited wealth.

options.text: A guarantee of never needing to work again.
options.note: The psalm assumes ongoing labor; it contrasts anxious toil with trust, not labor with idleness.

options.text: Extra hours added to each day.
options.note: The text speaks of sleep and rest given by God, not literally extended days.

options.text: As a heritage from the LORD and the fruit of the womb as His reward.
options.note: This is the psalm's exact, positive description of children as a divine gift.

options.text: As a burden to be endured for the family's sake.
options.note: The psalm frames children as a gift and reward, the opposite of a mere burden.

options.text: As a sign of excessive wealth.
options.note: The text ties children to the LORD's gift and reward, not to wealth as such.

options.text: As a purely private matter with no wider significance.
options.note: Verse 5 gives children public significance, standing with their father at the gate.

quiz.q: What image does verse 4 use to describe children born in one's youth?

options.text: Arrows in the hand of a warrior.
options.note: This is the exact image the psalm uses, picturing strength and purposeful defense.

options.text: Seeds scattered without purpose.
options.note: The image given is purposeful and strong, arrows in a warrior's hand, not aimless scattering.

options.text: Coins stored in a treasury.
options.note: The psalm's image is about strength and defense, not stored wealth.

options.text: Birds flying freely without direction.
options.note: The specific image is arrows aimed with purpose, not birds in flight.

quiz.q: How does Jesus echo this psalm's confession that only the LORD's building endures?

options.text: He declares He will build His church so that the gates of Hades cannot overpower it.
options.note: Matthew 16:18 is the direct New Testament fulfillment of this psalm's confession.

options.text: He teaches that human builders never need divine help.
options.note: This directly contradicts the psalm's and Jesus's own teaching on dependence on God.

options.text: He applies the psalm only to physical construction projects.
options.note: Jesus applies the building imagery to His church, a spiritual house, not merely physical construction.

options.text: He rejects the psalm's imagery as outdated.
options.note: Jesus draws directly on this very imagery rather than rejecting it.

questions: The psalm names three kinds of labor, building, guarding, and toiling for bread, all empty without the LORD. Where in your own work are you laboring anxiously as if it all depended on you?
Verse 2 contrasts anxious toil with the rest the LORD gives His beloved. What would it look like for you to work diligently but rest from anxiety this week?
Children are called a heritage and reward from the LORD, not merely an achievement or burden. How does this shape the way you view family, whether your own children or those in your life?

The image of arrows in a warrior's hand suggests children are shaped with purpose and aim. How are you intentionally aiming the next generation, whether your own children or those you mentor, toward Christ?

Jesus declares He will build His church so that nothing can overpower it. Where do you need to trust Christ as the builder rather than relying on your own strength to sustain your ministry, family, or work?

translationNotes.note: For He gives sleep to His beloved can also be rendered He gives to His beloved in sleep, meaning God accomplishes good for His people even while they rest, without their frantic effort; both readings teach the same trust.
Heritage, nachalah, is inheritance language elsewhere used for the land itself, Numbers 26:53, applying the same gift-language of a lasting, God-given portion to children.
The gate was the ancient city's place of legal and civic business; a man supported by many grown children could face disputes there without fear of being overwhelmed or shamed.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 127 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (5) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 127:1-5. One em dash in verse 2 was converted to a comma; no other em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's message 'Don't Eat the Bread of Anxious Toil' (on Psalm 127:1-2) at desiringgod.org.
